articles.label: Investigation
articles.headline: Escambia County's $2.3M tourism contract renewed without bid process
articles.dek: The Tourism Development Council extended its marketing agreement with a Jacksonville firm for the third consecutive year, bypassing competitive bidding requirements that county policy appears to mandate.
brief: Escambia County's Tourism Development Council voted 7-2 to renew a $2.3 million annual marketing contract with Jacksonville-based Beaches & Beyond Marketing without issuing a request for proposals.

County purchasing policy requires competitive bidding for contracts exceeding $50,000, but TDC staff cited a 2019 exemption for 'professional services' that county attorneys say was never formally adopted.
The firm has received more than $6.8 million from the county since 2023, with each renewal approved under the same disputed exemption.
articles.body:
The conference room on the third floor of the Escambia County governmental complex smelled like burnt coffee and carpet cleaner. Seven members of the Tourism Development Council sat behind a curved dais on the morning of April 16, paper agendas fanned out in front of them. Item 4B—renewal of the destination marketing contract—took less than four minutes.

No discussion of alternative vendors. No questions about performance metrics. No mention of the $2.3 million price tag, up 12% from the previous year.

The vote was 7-2. Beaches & Beyond Marketing of Jacksonville would continue promoting Escambia County tourism for a third consecutive year.

What the TDC did not discuss: whether the contract should have gone to bid in the first place.

The exemption that wasn't

Escambia County's purchasing policy is unambiguous. Section 34.7 requires competitive sealed bids for any contract exceeding $50,000. The tourism marketing agreement exceeds that threshold by a factor of 46.

TDC Executive Director Marcus Whelan told The Flightline the contract falls under an exemption for professional services established in 2019. He provided a one-page memo from then-County Administrator Jack Brown authorizing the exemption for "marketing, advertising, and promotional services requiring specialized expertise."

The memo is dated November 14, 2019. It was never presented to the Board of County Commissioners for approval.

"Administrative memos don't create policy," said Rebecca Torres, an attorney with the Florida Center for Government Accountability. "In Escambia County, purchasing exemptions require a formal resolution adopted by the commission. I've reviewed their code. There's no ambiguity here."

County Attorney Alison Yates declined to comment on whether the exemption is legally valid. She said her office is "reviewing the matter" but would not provide a timeline.

$6.8 million and counting

Beaches & Beyond Marketing first won the Escambia County tourism account in March 2023. That contract—$1.85 million annually—did go through a competitive RFP process. Three firms submitted proposals. Beaches & Beyond scored highest on a rubric that weighted "creative vision" at 40% and "local market knowledge" at 25%.

The firm is based in Jacksonville, 340 miles from Pensacola Beach.

Since then, the contract has been renewed twice without competitive bidding. The 2024 renewal increased the annual fee to $2.05 million. The 2025 renewal—approved last week—pushed it to $2.3 million.

Total payments to the firm over three years: $6.8 million, funded entirely by the county's tourist development tax on hotel stays.

TDC board member Sandra Castellanos cast one of the two dissenting votes on April 16. She said she requested a breakdown of the firm's deliverables before the meeting but never received one.

"I asked for visitor data, conversion metrics, anything showing return on investment," Castellanos said. "What I got was a glossy PDF with beach photos and the word 'engagement' used fourteen times."

Performance questions

The Flightline obtained Beaches & Beyond's 2025 annual report to the TDC through a public records request. The 28-page document highlights a 23% increase in social media followers across platforms and a "record-breaking" 4.2 million impressions on digital advertising campaigns.

What it does not include: hotel occupancy rates, visitor spending data, or any comparison to pre-contract benchmarks.

Visit Pensacola, the county's previous destination marketing organization, tracked all three metrics in its annual reports through 2022. That organization was dissolved when the county shifted to the contracted model.

Whelan said occupancy data is "proprietary" and collected by a separate vendor. He said visitor spending figures are compiled by Visit Florida, the state tourism agency, and lag by 18 months.

"We can't be held responsible for metrics we don't control," Whelan said.

Asked why the contract renewal didn't include performance benchmarks tied to compensation, Whelan said the TDC "trusts the expertise of our marketing partner."

Board composition

The seven TDC members who voted to renew the contract include four hotel industry representatives, as required by state statute governing tourist development councils. One of those representatives, Greg Hollander, is general manager of the Hilton Pensacola Beach.

Beaches & Beyond's 2025 report lists Hilton Pensacola Beach as a "featured partner" in three separate advertising campaigns.

Hollander did not respond to requests for comment. Whelan said board members with potential conflicts are advised to recuse themselves but are not required to do so under TDC bylaws.

"Mr. Hollander's property benefits from tourism promotion, as do all properties in the county," Whelan said. "That's the entire point of the TDC."

What happens next

The April 16 vote does not finalize the contract. Under county procedures, the agreement must be ratified by the Board of County Commissioners at a future meeting. That vote has not yet been scheduled.

Commissioner Lumon May, whose district includes Pensacola Beach, said he plans to ask the county attorney for a formal opinion on the bidding exemption before the ratification vote.

"If we have a policy that says contracts over $50,000 go to bid, and we're not following that policy, I need to understand why," May said. "The public deserves an answer."

Commissioner Jeff Bergosh, who chairs the board, did not respond to multiple requests for comment.

The Flightline submitted a public records request for all communications between TDC staff and Beaches & Beyond Marketing since January 2025. The county acknowledged the request on April 18 and estimated a response time of 14 business days.

articles.label: Development
articles.headline: Downtown Pensacola's empty storefronts hit five-year high as rent prices climb
articles.dek: Palafox Street saw 14 retail closures since January while asking rents rose 18 percent. Property owners say they're holding out for national tenants. Local business advocates say that strategy is killing the district.
brief: Downtown Pensacola now has 31 vacant retail spaces, the highest count since 2021's pandemic peak
Average asking rent on Palafox Street reached $28.50 per square foot, up from $24.15 last April
Local business owners report lease renewal offers 30 to 40 percent above their current rates
articles.body:
The cardboard covers the windows at 28 South Palafox. A handwritten sign taped to the door reads "Thanks for 7 great years." Inside, dust settles on the empty display cases where Coastal Curiosities once sold sea glass jewelry and locally made candles.

Three doors down, another vacancy. Two blocks north, two more. The pattern repeats itself along downtown Pensacola's main commercial corridor like missing teeth in a smile.

A Flightline survey of ground-floor retail spaces in the downtown core found 31 vacancies as of April 15—the highest count since the pandemic shutdowns of early 2021. Fourteen of those spaces emptied since January. The number represents roughly 22 percent of available storefronts in the district bounded by Main Street, Tarragona Street, Garden Street and the waterfront.

The rent question

Commercial real estate listings reviewed by The Flightline show average asking rents on Palafox Street now sit at $28.50 per square foot annually, up 18 percent from $24.15 in April 2025. Some premium corner locations list at $34 per square foot or higher.

For a typical 1,200-square-foot retail space, that translates to $34,200 per year in rent alone—before utilities, insurance, inventory or payroll.

"The math just stopped working," said Elena Vasquez, who closed her boutique clothing store on South Palafox in February after her landlord proposed raising her rent from $2,100 to $3,400 monthly. "I wasn't losing money at the old rate. I wasn't getting rich either. But the new number? I'd have to double my sales just to break even."

Vasquez now sells through an online storefront and a booth at the Palafox Market on Saturdays. She said her monthly overhead dropped by 80 percent.

Holding out for national brands

Property owners and commercial brokers tell a different story. They describe a market correcting after years of below-market rents and positioning itself to attract tenants with stronger financials.

Marcus Chen, a commercial broker with Gulf Coast Realty Partners who represents several downtown landlords, said property owners watched comparable Florida downtowns—St. Augustine, Delray Beach, Winter Park—command significantly higher rents while attracting recognized retail brands.

"Pensacola has always undervalued itself," Chen said. "You have property owners who kept rents flat for loyal tenants during COVID, during the hurricane recovery. Now they're trying to catch up to actual market value."

Chen confirmed that several of his clients have rejected lease offers from local entrepreneurs in favor of waiting for regional or national tenants. He declined to name specific properties or prospective tenants but said negotiations are ongoing with "multiple recognized brands" for Palafox locations.

"A Warby Parker or an Anthropologie changes the whole character of a block," he said. "That's the long game."

The long game's cost

Critics of that strategy point to the current reality on the street: a growing patchwork of dark windows that they say undermines the district's appeal to any tenant, national or local.

"Every empty storefront makes the next one more likely," said Robert Kinney, executive director of the Downtown Pensacola Business Association. "Foot traffic feeds on itself. When people see closed signs and papered windows, they stop coming. And when they stop coming, the businesses that are still open suffer too."

The association's own data shows pedestrian counts at three Palafox Street monitoring points dropped 12 percent between March 2025 and March 2026. Weekend counts held relatively steady. Weekday traffic—particularly during lunch hours—fell sharply.

Kinney said he's heard from multiple business owners whose landlords explicitly told them they were waiting for "better tenants."

"Better meaning richer," he said. "But a space that sits empty for two years waiting for some theoretical national brand isn't generating any rent at all. And it's driving away the customers that everyone needs."

Who's leaving

The 14 businesses that closed downtown since January span categories: two restaurants, three clothing boutiques, two gift shops, a bookstore, a jewelry store, two art galleries, a yoga studio, a specialty food shop and a home goods store. Nine cited rent increases in interviews or public statements. Three declined to comment. Two could not be reached.

Not all the closures trace directly to lease disputes. The owner of Pensacola Pages, the independent bookstore that closed in March after 12 years, said online competition and changing reading habits played the larger role. But she added that a proposed 35 percent rent increase "made the decision easier."

"I might have kept fighting for another few years at the old rent," said Barbara Tillman, the store's owner. "The new number made it clear my landlord didn't want me fighting."

New tenants, different model

Not every vacant space stays empty. Five downtown storefronts that turned over in the past year reopened under new tenants—but the pattern of what's replacing what concerns some neighborhood advocates.

Three of the five new tenants are service businesses rather than retail: a real estate office, an insurance agency and a law firm's satellite location. One is a high-end cocktail bar backed by out-of-state investors. One is a franchise dessert shop.

None are locally owned retail.

"We're losing the things that made downtown interesting," said Kinney. "The weird little shops, the places where the owner knows your name, the stuff you can't get at a mall or order on Amazon. That's what draws people downtown. Not another insurance office."

City's limited options

Pensacola city government has few direct levers to influence private commercial rents. The city does not regulate lease terms, and Florida law preempts local rent control ordinances.

City Council member Allison Michaels, whose District 6 includes downtown, said she's heard growing frustration from constituents but acknowledged the limits of what elected officials can do.

"We can't force a property owner to accept a lower rent," Michaels said. "What we can do is make sure we're not creating additional barriers—permitting delays, unclear regulations, parking problems that hurt businesses."

The city's Community Redevelopment Agency does offer facade improvement grants and has funded streetscape upgrades along Palafox. But those programs target property conditions, not lease economics.

Michaels said she plans to bring a resolution to the council in May calling for a study of commercial vacancy patterns downtown and potential incentive programs. She declined to specify what form those incentives might take.

Waiting out the strategy

At 28 South Palafox, the space where Coastal Curiosities operated sits empty three months after closing. The listing on LoopNet shows an asking rent of $31 per square foot. No lease signed yet.

Across the street, through a window without cardboard, Marisol's Café serves lunch to a half-empty dining room. Owner Marisol Hernandez said her lease runs through 2027. She hasn't seen a renewal offer yet.

"I try not to think about it," she said, wiping down a table that hadn't been used that day. "I've been here nine years. I thought I'd retire here. Now?" She shrugged. "I make good food. I hope that's enough."

Through the window behind her, two more storefronts stood dark.

articles.label: Economy
articles.headline: Blue Angels homecoming week brings $31M to Pensacola—and questions about who benefits
articles.dek: The July airshow fills hotels from Perdido Key to Gulf Breeze, but merchants in historically Black neighborhoods near NAS Pensacola say they've been cut out of the economic windfall for decades.
brief: The Blue Angels Homecoming Air Show generates an estimated $31.4 million in direct economic impact for the Pensacola metro area annually, according to Visit Pensacola data.
Business owners in Brownsville and Warrington—the predominantly Black neighborhoods closest to NAS Pensacola—say they see almost none of that spending despite their proximity to the event.
City and tourism officials have announced a new 'Homecoming Heritage Trail' initiative for 2026, though some community leaders question whether it will meaningfully redirect visitor dollars.
articles.body:
The parking lot at Sonny's Bar-B-Q on Navy Boulevard fills up by 9 a.m. on the Saturday of Homecoming weekend. Families in lawn chairs wait for the F/A-18 Hornets to scream overhead, close enough to rattle the windows of the strip mall. They buy bottled water from the gas station next door. They eat lunch. Then they drive back to their hotels on Pensacola Beach or Perdido Key.

Half a mile east, on the same road, Clarence Washington has operated Washington's Grocery for 34 years. He can hear the jets from behind his register. He watches the traffic stack up outside his window. Almost none of it stops.

"They come for the show, they stay at the beach, they eat at the chain restaurants," Washington said on a recent Tuesday afternoon, restocking a cooler of sodas. "I'm right here. I've been right here. But I might as well be in Mobile for all the good it does me."

The Blue Angels Homecoming Air Show, held each July at Naval Air Station Pensacola, is the region's signature tourism event. Visit Pensacola estimates the three-day spectacle generates $31.4 million in direct economic impact annually, with hotel occupancy rates exceeding 95 percent from Perdido Key to Gulf Breeze. An additional 85,000 visitors flood into Escambia County, spending money on restaurants, gas and entertainment.

But a growing chorus of business owners and community advocates in Warrington and Brownsville—the historically Black neighborhoods that sit in the shadow of the naval station's fences—argue that proximity to the airshow has never translated to prosperity. They say the economic windfall has flowed around their communities for decades, channeled by infrastructure decisions, marketing priorities and a tourism apparatus that treats their streets as a pass-through rather than a destination.

The geography of spending

NAS Pensacola sits at the southern tip of a peninsula, with the main gates opening onto Navy Boulevard and Blue Angel Parkway. Warrington, a neighborhood of roughly 15,000 residents that is 58 percent Black according to Census data, begins immediately outside the fence line. Brownsville, smaller and older, sits a few miles north along the same corridor.

Both neighborhoods developed in tandem with the naval station, housing generations of civilian workers and military families. Commercial strips along Navy Boulevard and Pace Boulevard once thrived on that relationship. But base consolidations, the rise of chain retail on the north side of town, and decades of disinvestment have hollowed out many of those businesses.

Today, visitors driving to the airshow from downtown Pensacola or Interstate 10 largely bypass these corridors entirely. The primary route funnels traffic down Blue Angel Parkway from Interstate 110, depositing cars directly at the West Gate without passing through any commercial district. Visitors staying on Pensacola Beach or in Gulf Breeze cross the bay via the Bob Sikes Bridge, also avoiding the historic Black business districts.

"The infrastructure itself tells people where to go and where not to go," said Dr. Maxine Patterson, a historian at Pensacola State College who has studied the area's development patterns. "These aren't neutral decisions. They're the accumulated result of 50 years of choices about what gets invested in and what gets left behind."

'We did everything they told us to'

Patricia Coleman owns Coleman's Soul Food Kitchen on West Fairfield Drive in Brownsville. The restaurant has operated for 12 years, serving fried chicken, collard greens and peach cobbler to a loyal local clientele. Coleman said she has tried repeatedly to tap into Homecoming weekend traffic.

She applied to be listed on Visit Pensacola's official restaurant guide. She was told the guide prioritized "visitor-facing" locations in "high-traffic corridors." She inquired about setting up a booth at community events during the airshow. She was directed to a permitting process that she described as prohibitively expensive.

"We did everything they told us to do," Coleman said. "Get the health permits, get the liability insurance, fill out the applications. And every time, there was another hoop, another fee, another reason it wasn't going to work."

Visit Pensacola President Darien Schaefer said the organization has worked to expand its restaurant guide in recent years and that any permitted food establishment in Escambia County can apply for inclusion. He acknowledged that outreach to historically underrepresented business districts "can always be improved."

"We're in the business of promoting the entire destination," Schaefer said in an interview. "That means the beaches, downtown, and yes, neighborhoods throughout the community. We're committed to making sure everyone has a seat at the table."

A new initiative—and skepticism

In February, Visit Pensacola and the City of Pensacola announced the "Homecoming Heritage Trail," a pilot program for the 2026 airshow. The initiative will create a marked driving route through Warrington and Brownsville, highlighting historic sites, restaurants and shops. Participating businesses will receive signage, inclusion in a dedicated mobile app and a small marketing stipend.

City Council member Teniade Broughton, whose District 7 includes much of the affected area, called the program "a meaningful first step."

"We're not going to undo 50 years of disinvestment with a single pilot project," Broughton said at a March council meeting. "But we have to start somewhere. We have to demonstrate that these businesses exist, that they're worth visiting, that the heritage of this community is part of what makes Pensacola special."

The program has a budget of $45,000, funded through the county's tourism development tax. Organizers expect 15 to 20 businesses to participate in the first year.

Some community members remain skeptical.

"I've seen initiatives come and go," said Deacon Raymond Lewis, a longtime Warrington resident and president of the Warrington Community Association. "They put up signs, they have a kickoff event, they take pictures for the newspaper. Then the money runs out, the attention moves on, and we're right back where we started."

Lewis said he would judge the program by its results: whether visitor spending in the neighborhood measurably increases, whether the participating businesses see sustained benefit beyond the airshow weekend, whether the city commits to continuing the program in subsequent years.

"Show me the receipts," he said. "Not the press releases."

The view from Navy Boulevard

On a warm April afternoon, Clarence Washington swept the sidewalk in front of his grocery store. A truck rumbled past, headed toward the base. The jets weren't flying that day.

Washington said he has no plans to participate in the Heritage Trail program. He doesn't expect it to change anything. He's seen too many promises over too many years.

But he keeps the store open. He knows his customers by name. He extends credit to families waiting on paychecks. He's part of the neighborhood in a way that no tourism initiative can replicate or replace.

"The Blue Angels are good for Pensacola," Washington said. "I'm not mad about that. I just wish Pensacola remembered that we're part of Pensacola too."

He finished sweeping and went back inside. The traffic kept rolling past.
